title: Foliage, Mini-World, and Blueprint Preview

video.note: Primary range covers adding trees to Foliage mode, density adjustment, Align to Normal, mobility, scale checks with the mannequin, paint and single instance placement, moving selected foliage, LOD/performance awareness, and play testing the world. Blueprint preview connection references 02:56:37-03:20:24, and final playable-space detailing references 04:57:25-04:59:31.

learningTarget: I can add environmental detail to a small Unreal scene and explain how foliage, props, and simple interaction prepare a scene for a playable mini-world.

bellRinger.prompt: What details make an environment feel alive or intentionally designed?

vocabulary.definition: Trees, grass, plants, and similar environment detail placed across a scene.
A placed object that adds detail, function, or story to a scene.
A focused improvement pass that adds believable detail to an environment.
The route a player is encouraged to follow through a space.
The area the viewer or player should notice first.
How many foliage instances appear in an area.
Differences in scale, rotation, color, or placement that make repeated assets feel natural.
A visual scripting asset used to create behavior or interaction in Unreal.
A volume or event that starts an action when something overlaps or activates it.
An area designed to be explored or used by a player.

slides.title: Lesson 16 - Foliage, Mini-World, and Blueprint Preview

assignment.title: Mini-World Environment Pass
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a wide screenshot of mini-world environment.
Google Drive link to a close-up screenshot showing foliage or prop detail.
Google Drive link to a screenshot or written description of simple interaction or Blueprint preview.
Short reflection explaining how the scene became more believable or playable.

exitTicket: What one detail made your mini-world feel more believable or playable?
